company.name: McKesson
company.description: McKesson distributes pharmaceuticals, medical-surgical supplies, logistics services, and health care technology services to pharmacies, providers, manufacturers, and care settings.

overview.title: Business Profile
paragraphs: McKesson is one of the core U.S. health care distribution intermediaries, with fiscal 2025 revenue dominated by pharmaceutical distribution and logistics. Its U.S. Pharmaceutical segment distributes branded, generic, specialty, biosimilar, and over-the-counter drugs to retail chains, hospitals, health systems, specialty practices, pharmacies, manufacturers, payors, and patients.

The Medical-Surgical Solutions segment distributes medical-surgical supplies, lab equipment, pharmaceuticals, logistics, biomedical maintenance, and related services to physician offices, surgery centers, post-acute care facilities, hospital reference labs, and home health agencies.

overview.title: Registry Fit
paragraphs: McKesson is best treated as a high-scale distribution and coordination company rather than a product manufacturer. The relevant replacement question is not whether an open project can instantly replace national wholesale distribution, but whether interoperable, auditable, locally operated supply-chain networks can reduce dependence on a few centralized distributors over time.

moatNarrative: McKesson's moat is primarily scale, regulated operations, vendor and customer contracts, distribution-center density, working-capital capacity, controlled-substance compliance, and integration into pharmacy and provider workflows. Fiscal 2025 U.S. Pharmaceutical revenue of $327.7 billion and Medical-Surgical Solutions revenue of $11.4 billion illustrate a business with enormous throughput and operating leverage.

The moat is not purely software defensibility. It depends on physical logistics, purchasing relationships, reliability, regulatory controls, and customer trust. That makes it resilient against simple software substitution, but exposed to narrower forms of decentralization where open inventory systems, regional cooperatives, and verified marketplaces handle specific commodity flows.
decentralizationNarrative: The strongest decentralization path is a federation of local health systems, clinics, pharmacies, and regional wholesalers using open logistics software, shared product catalogs, verified lot/expiry records, and auditable replenishment workflows. This could improve transparency and reduce switching costs without pretending that every regulated drug can move through a permissionless network.

Medical-surgical supplies are more decentralizable than prescription pharmaceutical distribution because many items are lower-regulatory-risk commodities that can be pooled, refurbished, locally stocked, or partly manufactured closer to demand. The regulated drug channel remains harder because chain-of-custody, controlled-substance monitoring, manufacturer contracting, cold chain, and payer workflows are central to trust and compliance.
company.snapshotNote: Queued from the May 25, 2026 S&P 500 market-cap snapshot ranks 101-125; market data reviewed against CompaniesMarketCap in May 2026.

moat.rationale: McKesson combines national distribution scale, regulated pharmaceutical logistics, supplier relationships, customer contracts, working-capital depth, and embedded provider/pharmacy workflows. The moat is strong, though not absolute for narrower commodity and software-enabled workflows.

decentralizability.rationale: Some inventory, replenishment, catalog, and medical-supply workflows can be decentralized through open logistics platforms and regional cooperatives, but prescription drug distribution has heavy regulatory, contractual, cold-chain, and controlled-substance constraints.

profitability.rationale: McKesson reported $3.295 billion of net income attributable to McKesson Corporation on $359.051 billion of fiscal 2025 revenue. Absolute earnings are large, but distribution economics remain thin-margin.

peRatio.rationale: Approximate trailing P/E based on CompaniesMarketCap's May 2026 share price of about $766 and fiscal 2025 diluted EPS of $25.72. This is a point-in-time approximation, not a live quote.

marketCap.rationale: CompaniesMarketCap reported McKesson market capitalization of approximately $92.08 billion in May 2026.

products.name: Pharmaceutical distribution
products.summary: McKesson's U.S. Pharmaceutical segment distributes branded, generic, specialty, biosimilar, and over-the-counter medicines and related products across pharmacies, providers, health systems, manufacturers, payors, and patients.
products.whyItMatters: This distribution layer is a major control point in U.S. health care: it determines product availability, inventory visibility, replenishment reliability, and compliance workflows for many pharmacies and care providers.
replacementSketch: A realistic replacement would begin as open logistics infrastructure for clinics, independent pharmacies, mutual-aid health networks, and smaller regional wholesalers rather than a direct national-scale substitute. Open inventory, lot tracking, expiry management, and API-based replenishment could reduce dependence on proprietary ordering portals and opaque stock visibility.

For regulated prescription drugs, open tooling would still need licensed operators, audited custody records, compliant controlled-substance handling, and integrations with manufacturers, health systems, and regulators. The near-term opportunity is interoperability and bargaining power, not bypassing law or safety controls.

alternatives.name: OpenBoxes
alternatives.summary: OpenBoxes is an open-source inventory and supply-chain management system built for health care facilities, warehouses, and distribution, with lot tracking, expiry management, shipments, and multi-facility stock visibility.

openness.rationale: The project publishes source code on GitHub and identifies itself as open source with no license fees or user limits.

decentralizationFit.rationale: Self-hosting, multi-facility stock management, and open APIs make it suitable for distributed operators, though it does not itself create a decentralized market or regulated distributor network.

readiness.rationale: OpenBoxes reports real-world use in health care and humanitarian supply chains and has an active release history, but adoption still requires implementation, data cleanup, and operational discipline.

costLeverage.rationale: Open source licensing, self-hosting, and avoidance of per-user software fees can lower coordination costs for smaller health networks, though physical distribution and compliance costs remain.

alternatives.name: OpenLMIS
alternatives.summary: OpenLMIS is an open-source electronic logistics management information system for health commodity supply chains, designed around shared implementations, APIs, and low-resource settings.

openness.rationale: OpenLMIS describes itself as open-source software with a shared global code base and public project repositories.

decentralizationFit.rationale: Its model supports multiple implementations and interoperability APIs, which fits federated public-health and regional supply networks.

readiness.rationale: OpenLMIS is purpose-built for health commodity supply chains, but it is more aligned with public-health and LMIC contexts than immediate replacement of U.S. commercial pharmaceutical wholesale operations.

costLeverage.rationale: Shared open-source infrastructure can lower duplicative custom LMIS costs and reduce dependence on proprietary systems, while licensed distribution and compliance remain separate cost centers.

disruptionConcepts.name: Federated health commodity exchange
disruptionConcepts.summary: A federation of licensed pharmacies, clinics, regional wholesalers, and public-health buyers could use open logistics systems to publish verified inventory, lot, expiry, recall, and replenishment data across a shared exchange while keeping custody with licensed local operators.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: The concept shifts some coordination power from a few national distributors and proprietary ordering portals toward interoperable local stock visibility and pooled procurement among licensed participants.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Decentralization matters through federated inventory publishing and multiparty coordination. Bitcoin is not required for regulated fulfillment, though payment channels could be used later for fast settlement between verified participants.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Participants run or connect open LMIS and inventory nodes, publish signed availability and demand messages, and match replenishment requests through regional buying groups or cooperative clearing hubs.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Licensing, product identifiers, lot numbers, expiry dates, shipment scans, and receiving attestations constrain false fulfillment. Auditable logs and cross-party reconciliation reduce spoofed inventory, but the model still depends on regulated operators and periodic audits.
failureModes: Licensed suppliers may refuse to expose inventory data if it weakens pricing power or contractual leverage.
Regulated drug classes, cold-chain products, rebates, and payer-linked workflows may remain locked into incumbent channels.
False stock postings or delayed updates could create patient-safety risks without strong operational controls.
adoptionPath: Start with non-controlled health commodities and independent clinics or pharmacies that already manage inventory pressure.
Connect OpenBoxes, OpenLMIS, or similar systems to a shared catalog, recall feed, and replenishment request workflow.
Add licensed regional wholesalers and cooperative purchasing agreements once data quality and audit practices are proven.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The model distributes visibility and matching across many licensed operators while keeping regulated custody local.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: Open LMIS systems and APIs support the coordination layer, but commercial incentives and regulatory integration are difficult.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: Technically plausible for commodity and public-health supply chains, but hard for full U.S. prescription wholesale flows because of contracts, licensing, rebates, and controlled-substance rules.

incumbentPressure.rationale: Could pressure opaque inventory and procurement workflows at the edge, but would not quickly displace McKesson's national scale or manufacturer relationships.

products.name: McKesson Medical-Surgical
products.summary: McKesson Medical-Surgical distributes medical supplies, health care solutions, distribution services, pharmaceutical services, and clinical resources to alternate-site care settings.
products.whyItMatters: Medical-surgical distribution is the more physically decentralizable side of McKesson's portfolio because many items are commodity supplies where stock visibility, group purchasing, local warehousing, refurbishment, and limited local fabrication can change buyer dependence.
replacementSketch: A credible replacement path would combine open inventory systems, regional cooperative purchasing, shared safety-stock pools, and transparent catalogs for clinics, surgery centers, nursing homes, and home-health providers. The result would look like a network of locally accountable supply nodes rather than one centralized distributor.

For selected commodity supplies, distributed production or refurbishment could supplement wholesale purchasing. The system would still need quality assurance, sterilization controls, traceability, recalls, and product-liability governance.

alternatives.name: OpenBoxes
alternatives.summary: OpenBoxes can support medical-surgical inventory, shipments, lot tracking, expiry alerts, and multi-facility stock management for health care and warehouse operators.

openness.rationale: The code is public and the project presents itself as open source with inspectable, modifiable, redistributable software.

decentralizationFit.rationale: OpenBoxes supports self-hosted and multi-facility deployments, making it a strong base for regional or cooperative supply networks.

readiness.rationale: The project is active and built for health care inventory, but replacing distributor services also requires procurement, logistics, contracting, and quality systems beyond software.

costLeverage.rationale: Open licensing and deploy-your-way hosting can reduce software lock-in and per-seat costs for smaller providers coordinating shared inventory.

alternatives.name: Open mSupply
alternatives.summary: Open mSupply is an open-source electronic logistics management information system covering procurement, warehousing, distribution, cold chain, and dispensing, with DHIS2 integration for logistics indicators.

openness.rationale: DHIS2 describes Open mSupply as an open-source eLMIS for logistics and health commodity workflows.

decentralizationFit.rationale: Its procurement, warehousing, distribution, cold-chain, and dispensing coverage can support distributed public-health or regional medical-supply networks.

readiness.rationale: It is presented as widely used in low- and middle-income countries, but U.S. alternate-site provider replacement would require localization, integrations, and compliance adaptation.

costLeverage.rationale: A shared open-source logistics platform can reduce duplicate software spend and manual reporting costs, though physical distribution economics remain material.

disruptionConcepts.name: Regional medical-supply cooperatives
disruptionConcepts.summary: Clinics, surgery centers, long-term care providers, and home-health agencies could form regional buying and inventory cooperatives that use open inventory systems to pool safety stock, coordinate replenishment, and negotiate commodity supply categories collectively.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: The concept weakens distributor lock-in by giving smaller providers shared purchasing intelligence, shared emergency stock visibility, and a path to operate local warehouses or contract logistics collectively.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: The core role is cooperative and federated coordination, not Bitcoin. A shared open ledger or signed audit log could support member trust, while payments can remain conventional unless faster settlement becomes useful.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Members publish demand forecasts, stock levels, expiry windows, and purchase commitments into a shared system; the cooperative aggregates orders, assigns replenishment priorities, and manages local buffer inventory.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Receiving records, lot and expiry tracking, member audits, and reconciliation between purchase orders, shipments, and inventory movements constrain false claims. Cooperative governance handles disputes and member sanctions.
failureModes: Cooperatives may lack the working capital, logistics discipline, or supplier terms that national distributors can provide.
Hospitals and clinics may prefer incumbent reliability during shortages even if open systems lower software lock-in.
Data sharing across competing providers can be politically difficult without clear governance.
adoptionPath: Begin with commodity medical-surgical supplies in one region and a small group of independent providers.
Use OpenBoxes or Open mSupply for shared stock visibility, replenishment triggers, and expiry controls.
Expand into cooperative contracts, emergency stock pools, and eventually local warehouse operations once service levels are proven.

decentralizationFit.rationale: Ownership and coordination shift toward regional provider groups rather than a single national distributor, while still using accountable operators.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: Open inventory, procurement, warehousing, and distribution systems already map well to the information layer needed for cooperative stock management.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: Commodity medical supplies are more feasible than prescription drugs, but supplier contracting, liability, and operational reliability are still significant barriers.

incumbentPressure.rationale: Could pressure pricing and switching costs in selected categories, especially for smaller providers, but national scale remains valuable for broad catalog coverage.

disruptionConcepts.name: Local fabrication and reuse loops for selected supplies
disruptionConcepts.summary: For selected non-sterile, low-risk, or reusable medical-supply categories, regional operators could combine open design files, small-batch fabrication, repair, refurbishment, and reuse tracking to reduce dependence on centralized catalog distribution.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: The concept does not replace regulated sterile supply chains wholesale; it narrows the distributor's advantage in categories where local production, refurbishment, or reuse can meet quality requirements at lower coordination cost.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Decentralized manufacturing matters through local production capacity and shared open hardware designs. Bitcoin is not central unless used for marketplace settlement or incentive-compatible quality deposits between independent fabricators and buyers.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Clinics and regional workshops coordinate demand, approved design files, material specifications, inspection results, and refurbishment status through shared catalogs and inventory systems.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Quality is constrained by approved designs, materials traceability, inspection checklists, batch records, receiving acceptance, and recall-capable inventory logs. Higher-risk sterile or implantable items should remain excluded unless certified processes exist.
failureModes: Regulatory and liability constraints may eliminate many medical categories from local fabrication.
Quality variation across local producers could create safety risks without strong certification and inspection.
Economies of scale may still favor centralized manufacturing for many commodity disposables.
adoptionPath: Start with non-clinical accessories, storage, fixtures, replacement parts, and reusable support equipment where safety risk is lower.
Add documented quality checks, batch records, and recall workflows before expanding into more sensitive categories.
Use regional procurement cooperatives to aggregate demand and certify local suppliers.

decentralizationFit.rationale: Local fabrication and reuse directly distribute production and inventory control, but only for a subset of suitable categories.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: Inventory and batch tracking are feasible, but certification, liability, and buyer acceptance are weak points.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: Feasible for narrow, low-risk supplies and accessories; speculative for broader medical-surgical catalog coverage because of safety, sterility, and regulatory requirements.

incumbentPressure.rationale: This would pressure only selected categories at first and would not displace McKesson's full catalog or logistics network in the near term.

summaryMarkdown: Prepared a new McKesson registry bundle with two products: Pharmaceutical distribution and McKesson Medical-Surgical. The strongest open alternatives are OpenBoxes, OpenLMIS, and Open mSupply; disruption concepts focus on federated health commodity exchange, regional medical-supply cooperatives, and narrow local fabrication/reuse loops.
